figures.caption: Table 2: The reference minimum and maximum scores for MuJoCo, Adroit, and AntMaze datasets.
Figure 6: Comparison of CPQL (ours) with CQL using alternative multi-step operators (Uncorrected N-step, Retrace, and Tree-backup) on MuJoCo locomotion tasks from D4RL.

Table 1: Results for MuJoCo locomotion, Adroit manipulation, and AntMaze navigation tasks in offline D4RL. * indicates reproduced results: (algorithm*) for all datasets, (score*) for a specific dataset. Bold numbers are the scores within 2% of the highest in each environment.
Table 7: Normalized scores under a comparable wall-clock budget.
Table 16: Results for MuJoCo locomotion tasks in offline-to-online settings. Bold numbers are the scores within 2% of the highest in each environment.
Table 15: Results for MuJoCo locomotion tasks. Bold numbers are the scores within 2% of the highest in each environment.

Table 14: Results for MuJoCo locomotion tasks. * indicates methods trained with 3M gradient steps as reported in original papers. All other methods are trained with 1M gradient steps. Bold numbers are the scores within 2% of the highest in each environment.
Figure 5: Performance of CPQL in MuJoCo locomotion tasks.

Figure 7: TD loss of different λ values
Figure 9: Normalized scores of different λ values
Table 4: Detailed hyperparameters of CPQL, where we conduct experiments on MuJoCo-Gym (“v2”) and Adroit and AntMaze (“v0”) datasets.
Figure 3: Comparing CPQL→PQL (ours) with several baselines for offline-to-online RL.

Table 18: Comparison of CPQL with different trajectory lengths on MuJoCo locomotion tasks. Same (α, λ) means that CPQL with n = 10 uses the same hyperparameters (α, λ) as n = 5, while Best uses the best (α, λ) tuned for n = 10.
